replies.author: @Micnificent1997
replies.text:
There are genuine reasons to not like the Rock…but most people in these comments you will find don’t use those reasons.

“He has a no fight clause” so does Stathom and Diesel…yet no one dislikes them as people. 

“He plays the same character” I can name a bunch of actors who either play the same role in or aren’t even actually great actors…but people love them. Sometimes an actor is type casted and they stick to it…but they dislike the rock for it. 

“He lies about steroids” Valid. Most people don’t care about steroids, just don’t act like you’re natty when you’re not. 

“He didn’t donate to Maui.” He did. Him and Oprah donated $10 million. Not a lot for the 2 of them…so you can argue that, but then you’re getting into the entire argument about what rich people do with their money…which will then get political…even though a majority of people using this argument probably didn’t donate a dime. 

Take Pedro Pascal. Ge is a good actor. Versatile. I think a lot of the complaints with him is the touching stuff…at first it was fine. He had anxiety and normally it was just a hand hold. Then came Fantastic 4…and it looked to be more than a hand hold. Him and Kirby looked intimate…and she is married so you know people wouldn’t like that. Which then made people look at the past stuff.

So whether there is actually something weird or not there…it still gives off those vibes and people will take those and run with it…even without all the information. As we can see when people complain about the rock

It seems to me that private property still exists in the US, and donations are voluntary. I don’t recall this country ever being communist. Plus The Rock and Oprah contributed 5 million dollars each, which is for some reason not enough for Americans. 
If we divide 60 millions by 357 mln population of States we get 17 cent per capita. Can someone explain why people feel entitled to demand more money from wealthy celebrities? Or has it somehow become a legal obligation for them to pay in such situations? Their effort delivered nearly $60 million to thousands of displaced Maui residents—money that materially supported survival and early recovery. They could have chosen to do absolutely nothing—no fundraisers, no speeches, nothing. They could have just relaxed in the sun without a care in the world. And yet, after stepping up, they still face backlash and wealth shaming. How does that make any sense? When I give a homeless person two cents or two hundred dollars, he doesn’t follow me around lecturing me on how much I should have given. It is my free will to give as much as I want to.

replies.author: @rcnelson
replies.text: Lots of people seem upset about that, but why? They likely raised more money than Joe Schmuck and Lois Loser would have. Jerry Lewis raised millions to fight muscular dystrophy and that was never held against him.

replies.author: @Clippy_Can
replies.text:
@rcnelson Jerry Lewis made his plea at a time when the average citizen had a great deal of money. It was a time of unparalleled personal wealth.
Oprah and Johnson made their plea at a time when the average citizen had a hard time getting by.
Jerry Lewis was famous, but he was never immensely wealthy. At the time of his passing, his personal fortune was less than Johnson makes for a single movie. Oprah is naturally even more famous.
Had Oprah covered the entire donation by herself, it would have equaled 0.73% of her wealth. Had Johnson covered the entire donation by himself, it would have been 2.75% of his wealth. You can naturally split the difference.
Had Jerry Lewis covered the Telethon donations by himself, it would have been 4900% of his wealth. This is much less plausible.
But if you don't get it, you don't get it.

With the way the tequila market is heating up, it’s not far-fetched to say he could cross the billionaire line in the next 5 to 10 years or maybe faster if someone comes knocking with a big acquisition deal.

Credit where it’s due though.. he’s one of the hardest working guys in entertainment. Even if his on-screen persona feels a bit off, and yes.. monotonous and predictable at time? he’s parlayed that consistency into a powerhouse brand. In other words, not the flashiest actor, but definitely a shrewd operator.

replies.author: @the_absurd_hero
replies.text:
​@friendlypup5650  It wasn't just "millionaires ask the poors to donate money." People forget details of a situation very easily, which is bad when situations are complex.

The Maui fires demonstrated a deadly incompetence by local authorities (for example, there are plenty of stories of citizens disobeying police orders to save themselves and the lives of their families; obeying authorities would have seen them dead). The fires should have been a rallying point for better local leadership, transparency, and policy reform. But The Rock and Oprah, among others, inserted themselves into the situation and hijacked the natural response from victims who needed financial support, sure, but who more importantly were demanding answers from their local leaders and getting none.

On top of that, the fires began (and continued) under suspicious circumstances. So it looked pretty bad when millionaires started to buy up the once-expemsive, now-cheap land for redevelopment and extra mansions..

The problem wasn't just that the Rock and Oprah asked people with far less means than themselves to donate money; their attention shifted focus away from serious matters of governance, subverting a much-needed democratic reckoning, and seemed to run cover for the disastrous local leadership.
